Several years ago Google created The Aristotle Project to discover what made some teams work so much better than others.
What do you think the answer was?
The Aristotle Project
Effectiveness was, surprisingly, not strongly correlated with how well the team members liked or knew each other.
It was, however, strongly connected to how well the leaders created ‘psychological safety’.
The study then identified the key ways leaders created this safety.
One way was by establishing widely accepted group norms, written and unwritten behaviors that guided relationships.
These norms were always connected to a greater sense of collective purpose.
The Aristotle Project – The Answer!

The Abilene Paradox was introduced by Jerry B. Harvey, Professor of Management at George Washington University in 1988
Groupthink can lead to:
Collective rationalization
Lack of personal accountability
Pressure to give acceptance
Bad decision-making
Serious ethical breaches
Insufficient creativity
Self censorship
Illusion of invulnerability
Low probability of success
Groupthink: psychological phenomenon that occurs within a group of people in which the desire for harmony in the group results in irrational or dysfunctional decision-making
Abilene Paradox is when groups make ineffective decisions that are contrary to what each of the group members individually believe because they don't want to 'rock the boat’
With groupthink, on the other hand, the individuals are unaware that the team's decision is wrong or risky
Difference between Groupthink and Abilene Paradox
Abilene:
Individuals know the idea is bad but don’t do anything to stop it from moving forward
Groupthink:
Individuals don’t know they’re making a bad decision
Ways to reduce Abilene Paradox
Speak up about what you really want!
Ways to reduce groupthink…
Set expectations when forming group: say that debate is encouraged and for everyone to be a critical evaluator
Avoid the leader stating a preference up front
Diversify teams
Ask for customer or user feedback
Use outside research
Appoint a devil’s advocate
Create safe environment for people to speak up

Micro vs. Macroeconomics Refresher
History
Adam Smith
Considered the father of modern economics and capitalism
Adam Smith was a professor at the University of Glasgow, Scotland. He defended the idea of a free economy, or free markets, in his 1776 book, The Wealth of Nations
According to Smith, economic liberty guaranteed economic progress. As a result, government should not interfere

“By pursuing his own interest he frequently promotes that of the society more effectually than when he really intends to promote it.”


-Adam Smith,        
The Wealth of Nations
The Invisible Hand: a metaphor Smith used to describe unintended social benefits resulting from individual actions
The unobservable market force that helps demand and supply of goods in a free market to reach equilibrium automatically
What five microeconomic principles guide an economic way of thinking?
As we learn about microeconomics principles today, be thinking of real-life examples that illustrate the concepts
#1 Incentives Matter
Incentive: any factor that encourages or motivates a person to do something
People respond to incentives in generally predictable ways
Rewards or punishments
Direct incentive (like prices)
#2 Scarcity Forces Tradeoffs
Society’s wants are unlimited, but ALL resources are limited (Scarcity)
Due to scarcity, choices must be made. Each choice has a Tradeoff: the exchange of one benefit or advantage for another that is thought to be better
Limited resources force people to make choices and face tradeoffs when they choose
AKA the “no-free-lunch principle”
Every choice involves tradeoffs
#3 Opportunity Cost & Utility
Everyone’s goal is to make decisions that maximize their satisfaction. Everyone acts in their own self-interest toward what will make them happy (Utility)
Opportunity cost: the highest value alternative that must be sacrificed to get something else
It’s only the “next-best” that matters
All choices involve costs
People choose something when they perceive the benefits are greater than the costs
Q: What do you give up when you make a choice? A: Your 2nd best option is your opportunity cost
#4 Thinking at the Margin
Aka Cost-Benefit Analysis
All-or-nothing decisions are rare. Most of the decisions made each day involve choices about a little more or a little less of something
Marginal cost: what you give up to add one unit to an activity
Marginal benefit: what you gain by adding one more unit
#5 Trade Makes People Better Off
By focusing on what we do well (aka specialization) and then trading with others, we will end up with more and better choices than by trying to do everything for ourselves
Markets do this and money help equalized out that transaction
Market: any arrangement that brings buyers and sellers together to do business with each other

Sammy Wammy is a food scientist based in New York who is on a mission to produce the most succulent S'mores the world has ever tasted. Below is a summary of her story.

When passing by in front of a food truck near Central Park, she suddenly got an idea: Is there anything better than a perfect S'mores while walking through central park in the evening? The next day, a company was incorporated, and her dream, Sammy S'mores, was underway.
Sammy hired 3 food scientists with the objectives of making the perfect S'mores. Everything was tested, from the density of the marshmallows to the crunch of the graham crackers.
Once the perfect recipe was found (about 1 month later), Sammy bought a food truck customized with all the necessary appliances required to make these perfect S'mores on the go.
Sammy paid for a city license to have access to parking space C4 near the park, which would serve as the location for her food truck.
Sammy spent $10,000 buying necessary goods (e.g., branded napkins, uniforms, etc.).
Sammy hired 2 college students to prepare and serve the S'mores in the food truck.

The summary above refers to many examples of the factors of production. Identify as many as you can and categorize them as being either land, labor, capital, or entrepreneurship (Hint: There is at least one example of each!)

Five Market Systems
Alexa Contreras
1. Perfect Competition
Buyers and sellers are so numerous and well informed that all elements of monopoly are absent and the market price of a commodity is beyond the control of individual buyers and sellers.
2. Monopoly

Market structure characterized by a single seller, selling a unique product that faces no competition as they have ownership of resources or copyright/patent, or the high starting cost drives others away.
Facebook was sued by the FTC for being a monopoly.
What is the FTC?

According to the government, Facebook Inc. (FB) was becoming a social media monopoly. In December 2020, the Federal Trade Commission (FTC) and 46 states sued Facebook, accusing the firm of buying up competitors—chiefly WhatsApp and Instagram—to liquidate competition in the social media industry.
This Photo by Unknown Author is licensed under CC BY-NC

Natural Monopoly

Monopoly made by limiting resources
Are diamonds really scarce?
The government restricts other postal services from delivering first class mail.
When the government limits competition
When monopolies are formed other than through legal prohibition
Some states only give access to one water or electric company to people.
WHY?
Copyrights, trademarks, patents are ways governments can limit the use of someone’s idea and create a monopoly through intellectual property
3. Oligopoly
A state of limited competition in which a market is shared by a small number of producers or sellers. A small number of large firms have all or most of the sales in an industry.
4. Monopolistic Competition

Many firms offer products that are similar but not perfect substitutes. Barriers to entry are low. Unlike a monopoly, these firms have little power to set or curtail supply or raise prices to increase profits. Firms typically try to differentiate their products to achieve above market returns. Heavy advertising and marketing is common.
5. Monopsony
An imperfect market condition in which there is only one buyer.
Harvard Business School professor Michael Porter created in 1979.
Used to analyze an industry’s
attractiveness
its potential profitability
the main sources of competition
One of the most popular and highly regarded business strategy tools.

Macroeconomic Concepts: GDP vs. GNP (called GNI sometimes)

Economists and investors are more concerned with GDP than GNP because it provides a more accurate picture of a nation’s total economic activity, regardless of who’s making the money inside the USA, and thus offers a better indicator of the US’s economy’s overall health.
Until ’91 US used GNP as overall indicator of economic activity
Under Pres Bush Sr. switched from GNP to GDP
USA GNP counts production by American residents, no matter where they’re living at the moment
An American worker in America would count toward GNP
An American worker living in Spain would ALSO count toward GNP
Q: would a Canadian citizen residing in the US with a temporary visa count toward US GNP?
Q: would that Canadian citizen residing in the US count toward US GDP?
GNP is a good indicator of how Americans are doing financially, but GDP is a good indicator of how the US economy is doing.
GNP related to citizenship of person producing services/goods
How big is the US GDP Right Now?
Closest without going over gets to pick from the bag of good and bad!
US Gross Domestic Product

The US GDP is a measure of the total value of goods and services produced and provided in the US. It is calculated by adding together private consumption, gross investment, government spending, and net exports.
The GDP is a key indicator for analyzing the health of the US economy.
The US GDP is the world's largest, accounting for 25.32% of the world's GDP.

How does a stock market work?
What is a stock?
Represents ownership of a fraction of a company
Stockholders elect a board of directors to oversee the company; voting power is based on the number of shares they hold
The total value of a company’s stocks is its market value
The total value of a company’s assets minus its liabilities is its book value
Over 90% of a stock’s value is from expectations of future earnings; a stock’s value may fall even if the company reports increased earnings
Types of Companies
Whenever you make an investment you should consider the company’s market capitalization and investment style
Market capitalization = market value
Investment style is determined by the company’s expected earnings growth relative to the overall economy
Growth companies are predicted to increase earnings faster than the overall economy (2-4% per year)
Because of this, growth companies’ market value will be much higher relative to indicators such as book value and current earnings; this makes them riskier
Investment Style
Growth investing
the most straightforward strategy for new investors
Easy to find companies with a consistent record of earnings growth and lots of expansion potential as they are the most popular investments
Value investing
Analyze poorly rated companies to find ones that have been undervalued: solid fundamentals, good growth potential, and a much cheaper entry price
It is must harder to do value investing successfully but some, like Warren Buffet, argue that it provides higher returns in the long run
Standard & Poor's 500
The S&P 500 Index or the Standard & Poor's 500 Index is a market-capitalization-weighted index of the 500 largest U.S. publicly traded companies
Dow Jones Industrial Average
The Dow Jones Industrial Average (DJIA) is a widely-watched benchmark index in the U.S. for blue-chip stocks.
Q: What is a blue- chip stock?
The DJIA is a price-weighted index that tracks 30 large, publicly-owned companies trading on the New York Stock Exchange and the NASDAQ.
Another index like the S&P but tracks a substantially smaller amount of companies
Q: Who chooses what companies make it onto the DJIA?
Q: What companies are on it right now?
NASDAQ vs NYSE
Differences B/w NYSE and NASDAQ
Chinese Stock Market
Shanghai Stock Exchange dates back to 1860, closed in 1949 when Communists took over, and reopened in 1990.
One of three stock exchanges in China (others are Beijing Stock Exchange and the Shenzhen Stock Exchange)
Unlike the U.S. and every other major stock market in the world, the Chinese markets are almost entirely off-limits to foreign investors
Can Americans Buy Chinese Stocks?
Yes, you can buy Chinese stocks in the US through several methods, including:
purchasing American Depositary Receipts (ADRs) that trade on US exchanges
investing in US-listed China Exchange Traded Funds (ETFs)
using a US broker that provides access to directly trade stocks on foreign markets like those in Hong Kong or mainland China.
Each method has different levels of risk and complexity, with ADRs and ETFs being the most straightforward for US investors.
Indian Stock Market
Most of the trading in the Indian stock market takes place on its two stock exchanges: the Bombay Stock Exchange (BSE) and the National Stock Exchange (NSE)
The NSE is extremely new, only having started to trade in 1994 (compared to the NYSE starting in the 18th century)
Though the BSE has 5,500 stocks, 500 constitute more than 90%
India’s markets are among the largest and fastest-growing in the world
Latin American Stock Market
Diverse but interconnected, representing economies from Mexico to Argentina.
For decades, much of Latin America had hyperinflation and political instability—not a prudent region of the world in which to conduct business.
Brazil Stock Exchange called B3 founded 1890, largest exchange in Latin America by market capitalization and trading volume
Chile -> founded 1893, one of the most stable and modern markets in the region, focused on mining, utilities, and finance
Columbia -> founded 2001 as a merger of regional exchanges. Active in equities, bonds, and derivatives
Peru -> founded 1860 with strong mining and commodities
Mexico -> Bolsa Mexicana de Valores (BMV) founded in 1894, second largest exchange in Latin America: key sectors are telecoms, banking, and energy
Argentina -> Founded 2017, prone to volatility due to economic and currency fluctuations
London Stock Market
London Stock Exchange Group-one of the oldest in the world: founded 1801 but origins trace back to 1698
2007 merged with Italy’s main exchange and that’s when they added the word Group

Financial Times Stock Exchange (FTSE or commonly called “Footsie”) is the dominant index for 100 top blue-chip stocks It’s a brand of stock market indices, not an exchange. It started as a joint venture between the Financial Times newspaper and the London Stock Exchange.
Best place for equity market liquidity, benchmark prices, and market data in Europe, central hub for international finance, connecting investors and companies from more than 60 countries
Government Bonds
A government bond is a type of debt-based investment, where you loan money to a government in return for an agreed rate of interest.
The federal government issues treasury bonds, while local and state governments issue municipal bonds
The 10-year Treasury note is currently at 4.13%

Pool your money with other investors & make investments collectively
The fund provider owns the underlying assets and then sells shares in the fund to investors

Feedback – Giving Best Practices
Authentic: can’t just say something positive if you don’t mean it because it comes off as ingenuine
Clear and specific and then move on
Timely (bad news doesn’t get better with time), allows the person to make tiny changes right away and course correct before it becomes a larger challenge
Person should be able to tell you’re giving the feedback because you care about their success and success of the mission
Focus on the behavior, not the person
Give feedback when you’re calm
Feedback – Giving Best Practices Continued
Sandwich approach (positive, negative, positive) too widely used…can be seen through, so not recommended. But not great to end on a bad note
Positive often given in front of others
Constructive maybe given in front of others
Negative given privately

Frame constructive: “you may want to try”, “what do you think about doing x”, “here’s something that you can strengthen for the future” so that it gives the individual latitude to take the feedback upon themselves
Often allow it to be self-assessed “how do you think you did on x?”
Ask why they did x, don’t assume
Feedback – Receiving Best Practices
View feedback as a gift, an investment of someone’s time into your future
Allow it to settle
Avoid defensive stance, don’t respond with explanation
Let the person giving feedback finish completely and try to listen deeply
Feedback is not an assessment on your whole worth as an individual
Ask follow-up questions about the feedback
Feedback – Receiving Best Practices Continued

Neuroscience: people tend to focus (obsess) on only the negative or constructive feedback. Train yourself to focus on the positive too because feedback-giver wants you to do more of that positive thing
Everyone can make improvements, even if the work was A+
Show appreciation for the feedback and the person taking the time to invest in you being the best you can be

Make a decision—is this feedback something I want to follow? how will I do so? Feedback doesn’t always have to result in a change- maybe you made a deliberate decision to do something despite knowing you were going to receive such feedback
Ask for it – easier to take when you’ve asked for it instead of waiting for someone to pounce it on you

Business Model Canvas
Alexander Osterwalder’s version (there are others slightly dif)
Lean startup template for developing new or documenting existing business models.
Visual chart with elements describing a firm's or product’s value proposition, infrastructure, customers, and finances
Infrastructure
Key Activities: The most important activities in executing a company's value proposition. An example for Bic, the pen manufacturer, would be creating an efficient supply chain to drive down costs.

Key Resources: The resources that are necessary to create value for the customer. They are considered assets to a company that are needed to sustain and support the business. These resources could be human, financial, physical and intellectual.

Key Partners: In order to optimize operations and reduce risks of a business model, organizations usually cultivate buyer-supplier relationships so they can focus on their core activity. Complementary business alliances also can be considered through joint ventures or strategic alliances between competitors or non-competitors.
Offering: Value Propositions
The collection of products and services a business offers to meet the needs of its customers.

A company's value proposition is what distinguishes it from its competitors. The value proposition provides value through various elements such as newness, performance, customization, "getting the job done", design, brand/status, price, cost reduction, risk reduction, accessibility, and convenience/usability.
What is Apple value prop?
Customers: Customer Segments

A company must identify which customers it tries to serve. Customers can be segmented based on their different needs and attributes to ensure appropriate implementation of corporate strategy to meet the characteristics of selected groups of clients.
Mass Market: There is no specific segmentation for a company that follows the Mass Market element as the organization displays a wide view of potential clients.
Niche Market: Customer segmentation based on specialized needs and characteristics of its clients. e.g. Rolex

Segmented: A company applies additional segmentation within existing customer segment. In the segmented situation, the business may further distinguish its clients based on gender, age, and/or income.
Diversify: A business serves multiple customer segments with different needs and characteristics.

Multi-Sided Platform / Market: For a smooth day-to-day business operation, some companies will serve mutually dependent customer segments. Visa credit card company will provide services to credit card holders while simultaneously assisting merchants who accept those credit cards.
This Photo by Unknown Author is licensed under CC BY-SA
Customers: Channels
A company can deliver its value proposition to its targeted customers through different channels.
Effective channels will distribute a company's value proposition in ways that are fast, efficient and cost-effective.
An organization can reach its clients through:
its own channels (store front)
partner channels (major distributors)
or a combination of both.
Which one of these do I use for my book sales?
Customers: Customer Relationships
Customer Relationships: To ensure the survival and success of any businesses, companies must identify the type of relationship they want to create with their customer segments.
3 types:
how the business will get new customers
how the business will keep customers purchasing or using its services
how the business will grow its revenue from its current customers.
Customers: Giving Customers Assistance
Personal Assistance: Assistance in a form of employee-customer interaction. Such assistance is performed during sales and/or after sales.
Dedicated Personal Assistance: The most intimate and hands-on personal assistance in which a sales representative is assigned to handle all the needs and questions of a special set of clients.

Self Service: The type of relationship that translates from the indirect interaction between the company and the clients. Here, an organization provides the tools needed for the customers to serve themselves easily and effectively.

Automated Services: A system similar to self-service but more personalized as it has the ability to identify individual customers and their preferences. An example of this would be Amazon.com making book suggestions based on the characteristics of previous book purchases.

Communities: Creating a community allows for direct interactions among different clients and the company. The community platform produces a scenario where knowledge can be shared and problems are solved between different clients.
Co-creation: A personal relationship is created through the customer's direct input to the final outcome of the company's products/services.
Finances: Revenue Streams
The way a company makes income from each customer segment. Several ways to generate a revenue stream:
Asset Sale – (the most common type) Selling ownership rights to a physical good. e.g. retail corporations
Usage Fee – Money generated from the use of a particular service. e.g. UPS
Subscription Fees – Revenue generated by selling access to a continuous service. e.g. Netflix
Lending/Leasing/Renting – Giving exclusive right to an asset for a particular period of time. e.g. Leasing a Car
Licensing – Revenue generated from charging for the use of a protected intellectual property.
Brokerage Fees – Revenue generated from an intermediate service between 2 parties. e.g. Broker selling a house for commission
Advertising – Revenue generated from charging fees for product advertising
Finances: Cost Structure
This describes the most important monetary consequences while operating under different business models.
Cost-Driven – minimizing all costs and having no frills. e.g. Low-cost airlines
Value-Driven – Less concerned with cost, this business model focuses on creating value for products and services. e.g. Louis Vuitton, Rolex
Characteristics of Cost Structures:
Fixed Costs – Costs are unchanged across different applications. e.g. salary, rent

Variable Costs – Costs vary depending on the amount of production of goods or services. (e.g. music festivals-more musicians you have playing, the most costs you’re incurring as the festival organizer)
Economies of Scale – Costs go down as the amount of goods are ordered or produced. (buying from your suppliers in bulk)
Economies of Scope – Costs go down due to incorporating other businesses which have a direct relation to the original product. (McDonald’s adding vegan burgers to their menu)

Fair Use
4 basic guidelines for using copyrighted work:
Purpose: No for commercial use. The use is for nonprofit, education, criticism, comment, news reporting, scholarship, or research. Favors transformative, not merely reproductions
Nature: The material is mainly facts (rather than creative expression),
Amount: Generally the more you use, the less likely you are within fair use. Small amounts are excessive if they take the “heart of the work”
Effect: Using the material is not taking potential sales away from the original work

Do you need to have all 4 factors?

Fair Use is a Balancing Test

To determine whether a use is or is not a fair use, always keep in mind that you need to apply all four factors. For example, do not jump to a conclusion based simply on whether your use is educational or commercial. You still need to evaluate, apply, and weigh in the balance the nature of the copyrighted work, the amount or substantiality of the portion used, and the potential impact of the use on the market or value of the work.

Not all factors need to weigh either for or against fair use, but overall the factors will usually lean one direction or the other. Also, the relative importance of the factors is not always the same. Your analysis should guide you to a conclusion.

Surplus
Consumer surplus is the difference between the maximum price a consumer is willing to pay and the price actually paid.
Producer surplus is the difference between the minimum price a producer is willing to accept and the price actually received.
An outcome is efficient when an allocation of resources maximizes total surplus.
Deadweight Loss

The loss of total economic efficiency—consumer and producer surplus—that occurs when the freely competitive market equilibrium is not achieved, resulting in reduced trade. It represents lost gains from trades that do not happen, often caused by market distortions like taxes, subsidies, price ceilings (e.g., rent control), price floors (e.g., minimum wage), or monopoly pricing.
Taxing specific goods leads to a deadweight loss, which reflects reduced economic activity.
Taxing goods with an inelastic demand (price doesn’t affect as much) (and/or supply) leads to a smaller deadweight loss.

Phillips Curve

The Phillips curve is an economic theory that illustrates the inverse relationship between inflation and unemployment. Developed by economist William Phillips, it suggests that when unemployment is low, inflation tends to be high, and vice versa. This relationship indicates that as economic growth occurs, inflation rises, leading to more jobs and lower unemployment. The curve visualizes this tradeoff, showing that if one rate increases, the other must decrease. Overall, the Phillips curve provides insights into wage behavior and economic cycles
Explanation of Economic Growth Raising Inflation

Demand-Pull Inflation: As the economy expands, the demand for goods and services rises faster than business can supply. When "too much money chases too few goods," companies increase prices, causing inflation.
Lower Unemployment: To meet the high demand, businesses increase production and hire more workers, which reduces the unemployment rate.

Barter involves the trade of a good or service in the absence of a commonly accepted medium of exchange.
Inefficient because it requires double coincidence of wants, which occurs when each party in an exchange transaction happens to have what the other party desires
A double coincidence is pretty unusual, which is why a medium of exchange naturally evolves in any exchange environment.
Types of Money
Commodity money: the use of an actual good for money
Historically the first medium of exchange in an economy
Commodity-backed money: money you can exchange for a commodity at a fixed rate
Solves transportation problem of using commodity money
Fiat money: money with no value except as the medium of exchange
No inherent or intrinsic value to the currency
Commodity Money versus Fiat Money
Commodity money
Links money to something tangible
Limits inflation
Fluctuations in the commodity value changes all prices.
New gold discovery leads to inflation.
Fiat money
Not backed with a commodity
Not subject to macroeconomic risk by changing commodity value
Subject to rapid monetary expansion and inflation
Practice What You Know—1
Today in the United States, the dollar ($) is:
intrinsically valued money.
fiat money.
commodity money.
commodity-backed money.
Practice What You Know—1
Today in the United States, the dollar ($) is:
intrinsically valued money.
fiat money.
commodity money.
commodity-backed money.
Unit of Account
Unit of account: the measure in which prices are quoted
Creates a common language and unit of measurement
Enables people to make accurate comparisons between items
Creates a consistent method of record keeping
Store of Value
Store of value: a means for holding wealth
Today, we have other options for holding our wealth.
Examples: bank accounts, savings accounts, stocks, bonds
Measuring the Money Supply (1/2)
M1
The money supply measure  composed of currency, checkable deposits, and savings deposits
Checkable deposits are deposits in bank accounts from which depositors may make withdrawals by writing checks.
M2
The money supply measure that includes everything in M1 plus money market mutual funds and small-denomination time deposits (CDs)
With savings deposits included in M1 as of 2020, the difference between M1 and M2 has become minor.
Measuring the Money Supply (2/2)
Debit cards:
Access checking and/or savings accounts
Credit cards:
Technically not a part of the money supply
Involve a loan made at the cash register
Bank deposits from elsewhere pay for the purchase.
Business of Banking
Banks have two important roles in the economy:
They are critical participants in the loanable funds market.
Play a role in determining the money supply.
Their function is to serve as a financial intermediary.
Deposits: primary source of funds
Loans: primary use of funds
Bank
Interest Rates on Bank Deposits and Loans
Banks charge more interest for loans than they pay for deposits. The difference pays the banks’ expenses and produces profits.
Bank Reserves (1/2)
Banks seek to loan out the money they have, in order to collect interest on the loans.
Reserves: The portion of bank deposits that are set aside and not loaned out, in order to meet withdrawal requests.
Reserves include both currency in the bank’s vault and funds that the bank holds in deposit at its own bank, the Federal Reserve.
The reserve ratio (rr) is the portion of deposits that banks normally keep on reserve: rr = reserves ÷ deposits.
Fractional Reserve Banking
Bank Reserves (2/2)
It is important that banks maintain sufficient reserves.
Bank runs occur when many depositors attempt to withdraw their funds at the same time.
To discourage banks from risking a run, the Federal Reserve pays interest on reserves.
This practice reduces the opportunity cost of holding some deposits as reserves instead of lending them out.
Before 2020, the Fed required banks to keep 10% of deposits on reserve.
Economics in It’s a Wonderful Life
It’s a Wonderful Life (1946)
This is the bank run scene.
During the Depression, bank runs caused the failure of many banks.
The government had to find a way to solve this problem.
Moral Hazard and the FDIC
Between 1929 and 1933, 9,000 banks failed.
In 1933, the U.S. government instituted federal deposit insurance through the Federal Deposit Insurance Corporation (FDIC):
Government program that insures your bank deposits.
Goal: Increase bank stability, decrease bank runs.
Created moral hazard situation:
Moral hazard: lack of an incentive to guard against risk
Moral Hazard Example
Type A bank:
Conservative, no risk, earns low returns on their loans
Almost never fails, but gives small returns on deposits
Type B bank:
Takes big risks in hopes of earning big returns
Fails often, but the surviving ones pay huge returns
Even if the bank fails, deposits are insured by the FDIC.
Where would you choose to deposit your money?
Banks do not mint money, but through their daily activities they help create new money.
Example:
You deposit $1,000 in bank.
The bank loans out part of that money (up to $900).
You can still get your $1,000 deposit back.
Someone else now has $900 from the bank.
New money is created!
Money Creation
Practice What You Know—3
Banks increase the money supply by:
printing (minting) money.
controlling interest rates.
lending out funds to borrowers.
storing the money of savers.
Practice What You Know—3
Banks increase the money supply by:
printing (minting) money.
controlling interest rates.
lending out funds to borrowers.
storing the money of savers.
The Federal Reserve
The Federal Reserve (Fed): central bank of United States
Its three responsibilities are:
Monetary policy
Central banking
Bank regulation
Roles of the Federal Reserve (1/2)
The Fed acts as a “bank for banks.”
It holds federal funds which are deposits that private banks hold on reserve at the Federal Reserve.
Banks keep reserves at the Fed because the Fed clears federal funds loans, loans between banks.
The interest rate on loans between private banks is called the federal funds rate.
Roles of the Federal Reserve (2/2)
The Fed also acts as a lender of last resort.
Discount loans: loans from the Fed to private banks
Discount rate: the interest rate on the discount loans made from the Fed to private banks
The Fed also serves as a regulator of individual banks.
Responsibilities include setting and monitoring reserve requirements.
Practice What You Know—5
Discount loans are:
loans offered by private banks at a lower interest rate.
cheap loans to individuals from nonbank businesses.
loans from the Fed to private banks.
loans private banks make to each other.
Practice What You Know—5
Discount loans are:
loans offered by private banks at a lower interest rate.
cheap loans to individuals from nonbank businesses.
loans from the Fed to private banks.
loans private banks make to each other.
Monetary Policy Tools
The Fed has several tools it can use to alter the money supply:
Interest on reserves
Open market operations
Includes quantitative easing
The discount window
Includes special lending facilities
Vocab Words
The Federal Reserve buys and sells government securities to control the money supply and interest rates. This activity is called open market operations.

Quantitative easing is a type of monetary policy by which a nation's central bank tries to increase the liquidity in its financial system, typically by purchasing long-term government bonds from that nation's largest banks and stimulating economic growth by encouraging banks to lend or invest more freely.

The discount window is a central bank lending facility meant to help commercial banks manage short-term liquidity needs. Banks that are unable to borrow from other banks in the fed funds market may borrow directly from the central bank's discount window paying the federal discount rate.
This Photo by Unknown Author is licensed under CC BY-SA
Bank Reserves and the Interest Paid on Reserves
It is no coincidence that the excess reserves climbed immediately after the Fed began paying interest on reserves.
Open Market Operations (1/2)
Open market operations: the purchase or sale of bonds by a central bank
Buys securities → increase the money supply
Sells securities → decrease the money supply
When performing OMO, the Fed typically buys and sells short-term Treasury securities for two reasons:
The Fed’s goal is to get the funds directly into the market for loanable funds.
The market for Treasury securities is big enough to where the Fed can buy and sell without difficulty.
Open Market Operations (2/2)
Quantitative easing: the targeted use of open market operations in which the central bank buys securities specifically targeting certain markets
During the Great Recession:
In late 2008, Fed injected almost $2 trillion worth of new funds.
The Fed bought long-term Treasury securities and other targeted types, such as mortgage-backed securities.
Quantitative Easing
The Discount Window and Special Lending Facilities
Discount window: Struggling entities can borrow money directly from the Federal Reserve.
Historically, the discount window was for banks.
Since the Great Recession, special lending facilities have provided loans to other firms in difficulty.
Private firms in the financial industry
State and local governments
Small businesses, hospitals, and nonprofits

SUPPLY
Wait, how do I read a supply curve again?
What three conditions must sellers meet in order for there to be supply of a good or service?
What three conditions must sellers meet in order for there to be supply of a good or service?
Desire to make the product
Willingness to sell the product
Ability to sell the product
Market supply is the sum of the individual quantities supplied
Supply = the quantity of a good or service that producers are willing and able to offer for sale at various prices
As prices decrease, the quantity supplied decreases
Price and quantity move in the same direction
The law of supply
The direct relationship between price and quantity is known as the law of supply
Why do price and quantity supplied move in the same direction?
2 reasons!
1. Production decisions by existing producers
In a market-based economy, every producer’s primary goal is to maximize profits
Firms earn profits based partly on revenue (the amount of $$$ received in the course of doing business)
More revenue? More profits!
So…when prices increase, the desire to make a profit leads producers to increase their production of goods
They expect their profits to increase as a result
Likewise, when prices fall, producers are likely to cut production
2. Market entries & exits
When the price of a good or service increases, new firms may enter a market because they see a potential for profit
For example, a building firm might enter the housing construction market to take advantage of rising home prices
Suppose the firm built 20 new houses at $500,000 each
This would increase the quantity of houses supplied at that price
The reverse can also happen when prices drop
Producers may exit the market, decreasing the quantities supplied at certain prices
How do supply and price interact?
Only a change in price causes a change in quantity supplied
Economists call this movement along the curve itself a change in quantity supplied
Demand and Supply Shifters
What factors cause the entire curve to shift?
Quantity Demanded vs. Change in Demand
A change in demand is NOT the same thing as a change in quantity demanded
Price is key when people are deciding what and how much to buy. But! other factors can influence demand as well
A change in demand is when the entire demand curve shifts to a new position on the graph
This one shows a change in quantity demanded
This one shows a change in demand
This describes a change or shift in a market’s total demand
When a factor other than price causes the quantities demanded at every price to change

Graphing changes in market demand

Both the decrease and the increase in market demand have caused the entire demand curve to move!
When this happens (when a factor other than price alters demand), we say the demand curve has shifted
Increase in demand shifts the demand curve to the right
Decrease in demand shifts the demand curve to the left

If demand or supply shifts, what happens to equilibrium price and equilibrium quantity?

We’re going OUTSIDE!

DEMAND SHIFTERS
What happens when demand meets supply?
Market equilibrium = the point at which the quantity of a product demanded by consumers equals the quantity supplied by producers
Quantity Demanded
Quantity Supplied
When producers set a market price that is above or below the equilibrium price, economists refer to this as disequilibrium
When the price is too low, shortages result (too many consumers chasing too few goods)
When the price is too high, surpluses result (too many leftover items that no one wants to buy)
Excess Demand
Equilibrium price of smoothies = $2.50
What if producers set the price at $1.50?
5,000 would be demanded, but only 1,000 would be supplied
This would lead to a shortage
What happens when the price isn’t “right”?
Rent Control = Price ceiling
Rent control regulations make is illegal to charge more than a specified monthly amount for rental housing
Imposing a rent ceiling (price can’t go above that line) below the equilibrium rent leads to excess demand
At the same time, the supply of apartments in the market decreases as landlords who are unwilling to rent at such low prices seek other ways to use their properties
This leads to a shortage of apartments
Minimum Wage = Price Floor
Here, the wage is the price you sell your labor for
When the minimum wage is set above the equilibrium market price (price can’t go below the line/floor) for unskilled or low-skilled labor, employers hire fewer workers
Companies might switch to automation or self-serve
More people are looking for jobs than there are jobs available
At the same time, a higher minimum wage creates an incentive for more people to enter the labor market
The result is a surplus of workers
